episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Arts, Culture & Digital Storytelling.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down Top-Notch, Bottom-Shelf and [Global News] Retired EPS officer Bill Clark sentenced for leaking confidential info to media, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Can rural artists build sustainable creative practices and reach audiences independent of major urban centers and institutional backing, or are the structural barriers to audience-building and resource sustainability still insurmountable? Topics include: Audience-building dynamics and algorithmic discoverability for rural creators, Live events and community gatherings as audience-development mechanisms, Indigenous storytelling, language archiving, and community-controlled digital governance (Mukurtu, Tŝilhqot'in model), Funding models: donations, grants, co-ops, and infrastructure classification, AI-assisted production tools as capacity multipliers without editorial risk.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Central Cariboo Arts & Culture Society

articles.title: Tod Maffin - Official Web Site
articles.summary: Canadian culture commentator, agency owner, business journalist.

discussion.central_question: Can rural artists build sustainable creative practices and reach audiences independent of major urban centers and institutional backing, or are the structural barriers to audience-building and resource sustainability still insurmountable?
discussion.riley_position: Digital tools have fundamentally changed the economics of audience-building, and platform-independent distribution models (direct email, community radio, live events, cooperative infrastructure) can enable rural creators to reach audiences without relying on algorithmic amplification or institutional credibility.
riley_key_evidence: Tod Maffin built a 10 million weekly reach audience independently via newsletter and podcast after leaving CBC
Tŝilhqot'in Nation uses community screening events combined with online release to create distribution leverage
Cariboo Imagine initiative demonstrates shared infrastructure without requiring new institutions or mergers
discussion.casey_position: The accessibility of tools masks persistent structural barriers: rural creators face a cold-start problem with no local audience base to bootstrap from, local/specific content doesn't perform well algorithmically, live events don't scale geographically, and cooperative/donation models lack sustainable funding at regional scale.
casey_key_evidence: Most successful independent creators are city-based writing about national/international topics with high search volume
A Cariboo regional publication drawing from 65,000 people has a fundamentally smaller donor pool than national outlets
Resource-constrained arts organizations already operating at capacity can't add production responsibilities to shared platforms
discussion.resolution: Riley and Casey converged on 'governance before gear'—both agreed that the core issue isn't technology access but ownership structure and sustainability models. They aligned that community-controlled archives and projects designed with communal production processes matter more than platform choice, and that treating cultural infrastructure as permanent (like roads or CBC) rather than project-funded is the real structural shift needed.
topics_covered: Audience-building dynamics and algorithmic discoverability for rural creators
Live events and community gatherings as audience-development mechanisms
Indigenous storytelling, language archiving, and community-controlled digital governance (Mukurtu, Tŝilhqot'in model)
Funding models: donations, grants, co-ops, and infrastructure classification
AI-assisted production tools as capacity multipliers without editorial risk
